title: Why Is My Website Not Showing on Google?
meta_title: Why Is My Website Not Showing on Google? 8 Real Causes
meta_description: Wondering why is my website not showing on Google? Here's how to check indexing, noindex tags and rankings yourself, and what to fix first, step by step.
keyword: why is my website not showing on google
intro: You have paid for a website, it looks good, and it is nowhere to be found in search. Below are the actual causes, in the order worth checking them, and how to test each one yourself before you spend a cent.
body_html:
First, work out which problem you actually have

"Why is my website not showing on Google?" usually means one of two very different things, and the fix for each looks nothing like the other. Either Google does not have your pages in its index at all, or it has them and is choosing to rank other sites above you. Sort that out before you change anything.

Open Google and search site:yourdomain.com.au with no space after the colon. If you get results, you are indexed. If you get nothing back, you are not, and the first half of this page is where to look. If you do get results but still cannot find yourself for the terms you care about, skip to the ranking section.

Reasons your site is not in the index at all

The site is genuinely too new

A new domain with nothing linking to it can take anywhere from a few days to a few weeks to be discovered. You cannot pay to skip that queue, but you can speed discovery up: verify the site in Google Search Console, submit an XML sitemap, and use the URL Inspection tool to request indexing on your most important pages.

You are accidentally telling Google to stay away

This is a very common cause of a site that never appears, and it is usually an oversight. During a build, a noindex instruction gets added so the half-finished site is not picked up, then nobody removes it at launch. In WordPress it is one tick box under Settings then Reading, labelled something like "Discourage search engines from indexing this site". Check that first. Then view your page source and search for "noindex", and load yourdomain.com.au/robots.txt to confirm it does not say Disallow: /.

Your pages nominate somebody else's URL

A canonical tag tells Google which address is the real version of a page. When a site is built on a staging domain or copied from a template, that tag is sometimes left pointing at the staging address or at the original template site, so every page politely nominates a different URL as the one worth indexing. View the source of a page and look for the canonical link — it should point at that page's own live address. The same logic applies when a site answers on several addresses at once: with www and without it, http as well as https. Pick one, and redirect the others to it.

Google cannot crawl or render your pages

Sites built entirely in JavaScript, pages sitting behind a login, and content that only loads after a click can all be effectively invisible to a crawler even though they look perfect to you. The URL Inspection tool in Search Console shows the page as Google actually fetched it, which is the only version that counts.

Nothing links to the page

Google finds pages by following links. A page that is not in your menu, not in your sitemap and not linked from anywhere else can sit there unnoticed indefinitely. Orphan pages turn up on sites that have been rebuilt or migrated, because the old navigation went and nothing replaced the link.

Reasons you are indexed but still invisible

You are testing terms you were never going to rank for

It is a common instinct to test with a broad, brutally competitive phrase like "plumber" or "accountant". Those results are contested by national brands and directories. Search your business name instead. If you come up first for that, you are indexed and the real issue is competitiveness, not a fault.

Worth knowing too: Google personalises results by location and history, so what you see logged in on your office wi-fi is not what a customer three suburbs away sees. An incognito window is a rough sanity check.

No page on your site is actually about the search

If someone searches "emergency electrician Fremantle" and your site has a single Services page listing fourteen things as bullet points, there is nothing specific for Google to match. Ranking usually requires a page that answers one question properly. A thin brochure site will struggle against a competitor that has covered the same topics in real depth, because that competitor has a page aimed squarely at each search while you have one page trying to cover all of them at once.

Nobody links to you

Links from other sites remain one of the strongest signals Google uses to decide who to trust. A new site with no coverage, no listings and no mentions anywhere is starting from zero. That is slow, steady work, and anyone offering to fix it in a fortnight is selling you links you do not want.

You have no Google Business Profile

For anything local, the map results sit above the normal listings, and you cannot appear there without a verified Google Business Profile. It is free. Claim it, complete every field, choose the right categories, add real photos, and keep your business name, address and phone consistent everywhere they appear online. Because the map block sits above the ordinary results, a local business without a profile is absent from the part of the page a nearby searcher looks at first — and claiming it costs nothing but the time it takes to verify.

What to do, in order

  • Run the site: search to confirm whether you are indexed at all.
  • Set up Google Search Console. It is free, and it tells you directly which pages are excluded and why.
  • Remove any noindex tag or robots.txt block, check where your canonical tags point, then request indexing.
  • Claim and complete your Google Business Profile.
  • Read the Performance report to see what you already get impressions for. Those terms are usually the fastest wins.
  • Then, and only then, start building pages that target what your customers actually type.

The first four are things you can do yourself this afternoon, and they cost nothing. If you get through them and still cannot see any movement, the problem is competitiveness rather than plumbing, and that takes months of consistent content and link work rather than a one-off fix.

faqs.q: How long does it take for a new website to show up on Google?
faqs.a: Discovery and indexing typically takes anywhere from a few days to a few weeks for a brand new domain, and it is faster if you verify the site in Google Search Console and submit a sitemap. Being indexed is not the same as ranking, though. Appearing for anything competitive normally takes months of content and link building, and any timeline shorter than that should be treated with suspicion.

faqs.q: How do I check whether my website is indexed by Google?
faqs.a: Search Google for site:yourdomain.com.au with no space after the colon. Any results means Google has your pages; no results means it does not. For a proper answer, verify the site in Google Search Console and use the URL Inspection tool, which tells you page by page whether a URL is indexed and, if it is not, the exact reason.

faqs.q: Why does my site appear when I search my business name but nothing else?
faqs.a: That is the normal pattern for a site that is indexed but has no ranking strength yet. Your business name is a term nobody else is competing for, so you win it by default. Broader terms need a page specifically about that topic, enough depth to be genuinely useful, and links from other sites before Google will place you anywhere near the first page.

faqs.q: How much does it cost to fix a site that is not showing on Google?
faqs.a: Our SEO plans are $750, $1,500 and $2,500 per month, all GST inclusive and with no lock-in contract. If the underlying website is the problem rather than the search setup, a build is $750 one-off for the Starter tier and $1,500 for Business, with a custom tier quoted on the work. Several of the checks on this page cost nothing at all — the site: search, Google Search Console, the noindex check and your Google Business Profile are all free, and they are worth doing before you engage anyone.

faqs.q: Can you guarantee first page rankings on Google?
faqs.a: No, and neither can anyone else. Nobody controls Google's algorithm, and any agency guaranteeing a position is either targeting terms nobody searches for or using tactics that risk a penalty. Our SEO plans run month to month with no lock-in contract, so if it is not working for you, you are free to stop.
